content.text: Document understanding is the task of extracting structured information from visually rich documents — invoices, receipts, forms, contracts, research papers — where meaning depends on both the text content and its spatial layout. A simple NLP model operating on plain text loses critical signals: whether "$142" and "Total" are on the same line, whether a cell is a table header or a body cell, or whether two paragraphs are siblings or a nested list.
(LayoutLM family) that accept pre-extracted text with bounding boxes, and
(Donut, Nougat) that read the document image directly and generate structured output end-to-end.

attrs.alt: Document understanding pipeline comparison: OCR-dependent (LayoutLMv3) vs OCR-free (Donut) approaches, with model comparison table

content.text: The LayoutLM family (Microsoft, 2020–2022) extends BERT by adding two extra embeddings to each text token: a 2D positional embedding encoding the token's bounding box (x0, y0, x1, y1 normalised to [0, 1000]), and optionally a visual feature from the document image patch at that position. All three signals — text, layout, and image — are fused in a shared transformer.
LayoutLMv3: Unified Text, Layout, and Image Encoder
LayoutLMv3 (2022) is the most capable member of the family. It unifies three input streams in a single transformer with no modality-specific heads:
: WordPiece tokens from OCR output, each augmented with a 2D bounding-box embedding (4 values: x0, y0, x1, y1) normalised to 1000 units.
: the document image is divided into 16×16 patches, each linearly projected to match the text embedding dimension. Patch embeddings share the same positional embedding space as text tokens.

: Masked Language Modelling (MLM) on text tokens, Masked Image Modelling (MIM) on image patches, and Word-Patch Alignment (WPA) — predicting whether an image patch and a text word are aligned (same document region).

from transformers import LayoutLMv3Processor, LayoutLMv3ForTokenClassification
from PIL import Image
import torch

# Load processor (handles OCR + image preprocessing) and model
processor = LayoutLMv3Processor.from_pretrained("microsoft/layoutlmv3-base", apply_ocr=True)
model = LayoutLMv3ForTokenClassification.from_pretrained(
    "microsoft/layoutlmv3-base",
    num_labels=7,   # e.g. B-HEADER, I-HEADER, B-QUESTION, I-QUESTION, B-ANSWER, I-ANSWER, O
)

# Load a form image; processor runs Tesseract OCR internally (apply_ocr=True)
image = Image.open("invoice.png").convert("RGB")
encoding = processor(image, return_tensors="pt")

# encoding contains: input_ids, attention_mask, bbox, pixel_values
print("tokens:", encoding["input_ids"].shape)       # (1, seq_len)
print("bboxes:", encoding["bbox"].shape)             # (1, seq_len, 4)  ← [x0,y0,x1,y1] × 1000
print("patches:", encoding["pixel_values"].shape)   # (1, 3, 224, 224)

with torch.inference_mode():
    outputs = model(**encoding)

# Each text token gets a label: token classification for NER / key-value extraction
logits = outputs.logits          # (1, seq_len, num_labels)
predictions = logits.argmax(-1)  # (1, seq_len)
print("label ids:", predictions[0].tolist())

The standard fine-tuning task is sequence labelling with BIO tags. Each OCR token is labelled as B-KEY, I-KEY, B-VALUE, I-VALUE, or O. Post-processing pairs each KEY span with the closest following VALUE span.

from datasets import Dataset
from transformers import LayoutLMv3ForTokenClassification, TrainingArguments, Trainer
from transformers import LayoutLMv3Processor
import torch

LABEL2ID = {"O": 0, "B-KEY": 1, "I-KEY": 2, "B-VALUE": 3, "I-VALUE": 4}
ID2LABEL = {v: k for k, v in LABEL2ID.items()}

processor = LayoutLMv3Processor.from_pretrained("microsoft/layoutlmv3-base", apply_ocr=False)
model = LayoutLMv3ForTokenClassification.from_pretrained(
    "microsoft/layoutlmv3-base",
    id2label=ID2LABEL,
    label2id=LABEL2ID,
)

def preprocess(example):
    # example: {"image": PIL, "words": [...], "boxes": [...], "labels": [...]}
    encoding = processor(
        example["image"],
        text=example["words"],
        boxes=example["boxes"],      # already normalised to [0,1000]
        word_labels=example["labels"],
        truncation=True,
        padding="max_length",
        max_length=512,
        return_tensors="pt",
    )
    return {k: v.squeeze(0) for k, v in encoding.items()}

training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="layoutlmv3-invoice-kv",
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    num_train_epochs=5,
    learning_rate=1e-5,
    fp16=True,
    evaluation_strategy="epoch",
    save_strategy="epoch",
    load_best_model_at_end=True,
)

# trainer = Trainer(model=model, args=training_args, train_dataset=..., eval_dataset=...)
# trainer.train()

Donut (Document Understanding Transformer, 2022) removes the OCR step entirely. A Swin Transformer encoder processes the document image directly as a grid of patches, and a BART-style autoregressive decoder generates structured JSON conditioned on a task prompt. There is no explicit text detection or recognition — the decoder reads the document like a visual language model.
The output format is a JSON string that encodes the extracted information. For a receipt, the model might produce
without ever running an OCR engine.

import re
import json
from PIL import Image
from transformers import DonutProcessor, VisionEncoderDecoderModel
import torch

# Load Donut fine-tuned on CORD (receipt understanding)
processor = DonutProcessor.from_pretrained("naver-clova-ix/donut-base-finetuned-cord-v2")
model = VisionEncoderDecoderModel.from_pretrained("naver-clova-ix/donut-base-finetuned-cord-v2")
device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
model = model.to(device)

image = Image.open("receipt.jpg").convert("RGB")
# Donut resizes to 1280×960 and normalises
pixel_values = processor(image, return_tensors="pt").pixel_values.to(device)

# Task prompt: tells the decoder what to generate
task_prompt = "<s_cord-v2>"
decoder_input_ids = processor.tokenizer(
    task_prompt, add_special_tokens=False, return_tensors="pt"
).input_ids.to(device)

with torch.inference_mode():
    outputs = model.generate(
        pixel_values,
        decoder_input_ids=decoder_input_ids,
        max_length=model.decoder.config.max_position_embeddings,
        bad_words_ids=[[processor.tokenizer.unk_token_id]],
        return_dict_in_generate=True,
    )

# Decode and convert special tokens back to JSON
sequence = processor.batch_decode(outputs.sequences)[0]
sequence = sequence.replace(processor.tokenizer.eos_token, "").replace(processor.tokenizer.pad_token, "")
sequence = re.sub(r"<.*?>", "", sequence, count=1).strip()   # strip task prompt

# Donut uses XML-like special tokens; processor.token2json converts them
result = processor.token2json(sequence)
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
When OCR accuracy is a bottleneck (handwriting, low-quality scans, unusual fonts) — errors introduced by OCR propagate through the entire pipeline.
When you control the document domain and can fine-tune on a labelled sample — Donut fine-tuned on 800 receipt images matches specialized OCR+NLP pipelines.
When deployment simplicity matters — one model, one inference call, no external OCR API dependency.

TrOCR (2021) is a specialised model for the pure text recognition step. It uses a ViT image encoder pre-trained on document images and a RoBERTa/GPT-2 decoder pre-trained on large text corpora. Given a cropped text-line image, TrOCR generates the transcribed text character by character. It achieves state-of-the-art on both printed and handwritten text recognition.

from transformers import TrOCRProcessor, VisionEncoderDecoderModel
from PIL import Image
import torch

# Two checkpoints: printed text and handwritten text
PRINTED  = "microsoft/trocr-base-printed"
HANDWRIT = "microsoft/trocr-large-handwritten"

processor = TrOCRProcessor.from_pretrained(PRINTED)
model = VisionEncoderDecoderModel.from_pretrained(PRINTED)

# TrOCR expects a cropped single-line text image (not a full page)
image = Image.open("text_line.png").convert("RGB")
pixel_values = processor(images=image, return_tensors="pt").pixel_values

with torch.inference_mode():
    generated_ids = model.generate(pixel_values)

text = processor.batch_decode(generated_ids, skip_special_tokens=True)[0]
print(text)

# For full-page documents, run a text detection model first (e.g. DBNet, CRAFT)
# to get bounding boxes, crop each line, then run TrOCR on each crop.

Large multimodal models like GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet can answer questions about documents zero-shot, without fine-tuning or OCR. For many ad-hoc extraction tasks on diverse document types this is the fastest path to a working system, even if the per-query cost is higher than a specialized model.
Tables are especially hard: a naive OCR run loses the row-column structure. The best approaches depend on document type:
Parse PDF coordinate stream; exact, fast

# Using Microsoft Table Transformer for scanned document table detection
from transformers import AutoImageProcessor, TableTransformerForObjectDetection
from PIL import Image
import torch

processor = AutoImageProcessor.from_pretrained("microsoft/table-transformer-detection")
model = TableTransformerForObjectDetection.from_pretrained("microsoft/table-transformer-detection")

image = Image.open("scanned_report.png").convert("RGB")
inputs = processor(images=image, return_tensors="pt")
with torch.inference_mode():
    outputs = model(**inputs)

# Post-process: get bounding boxes with confidence > 0.9
results = processor.post_process_object_detection(
    outputs,
    threshold=0.9,
    target_sizes=torch.tensor([image.size[::-1]]),
)[0]

for score, label, box in zip(results["scores"], results["labels"], results["boxes"]):
    label_name = model.config.id2label[label.item()]
    xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax = [round(c, 1) for c in box.tolist()]
    print(f"{label_name}: {score:.2f} @ [{xmin},{ymin},{xmax},{ymax}]")

# Crop each detected table region and run structure recognition
structure_model = TableTransformerForObjectDetection.from_pretrained(
    "microsoft/table-transformer-structure-recognition"
)
# This second model labels cells, rows, column headers within the cropped table
ANLS (Average Normalised Levenshtein Similarity) is the standard DocVQA metric. It measures string similarity between predicted and ground-truth answers on a [0, 1] scale —
for exact match, and values below 0.5 are treated as wrong to avoid rewarding partial hallucinations.
is the normalised Levenshtein distance between the ground-truth answer

A production document understanding pipeline typically combines multiple components. Here is a practical recipe for invoice processing that uses pdfplumber for native-text PDFs and falls back to LayoutLMv3 for scanned images:

import pdfplumber
from PIL import Image
from pdf2image import convert_from_path
from transformers import LayoutLMv3Processor, LayoutLMv3ForSequenceClassification
import torch
import io

def has_text_layer(pdf_path: str) -> bool:
    """Returns True if the PDF has a native text layer (not scanned)."""
    with pdfplumber.open(pdf_path) as pdf:
        text = "".join(page.extract_text() or "" for page in pdf.pages)
    return len(text.strip()) > 50

def extract_with_pdfplumber(pdf_path: str) -> dict:
    """Fast extraction for native-text PDFs."""
    result = {}
    with pdfplumber.open(pdf_path) as pdf:
        for page in pdf.pages:
            tables = page.extract_tables()
            words  = page.extract_words(x_tolerance=3, y_tolerance=3)
            result[f"page_{page.page_number}"] = {
                "words": [w["text"] for w in words],
                "tables": tables,
            }
    return result

# Lazy-load model only when needed for scanned docs
_processor = None
_model = None

def get_layoutlmv3():
    global _processor, _model
    if _processor is None:
        _processor = LayoutLMv3Processor.from_pretrained(
            "microsoft/layoutlmv3-base", apply_ocr=True
        )
        _model = LayoutLMv3ForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
            "microsoft/layoutlmv3-base"
        )
    return _processor, _model

def process_document(pdf_path: str) -> dict:
    if has_text_layer(pdf_path):
        return {"method": "pdfplumber", "data": extract_with_pdfplumber(pdf_path)}
    # Scanned: convert first page to image and run LayoutLMv3
    pages = convert_from_path(pdf_path, dpi=200, first_page=1, last_page=1)
    image = pages[0].convert("RGB")
    processor, model = get_layoutlmv3()
    encoding = processor(image, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.inference_mode():
        outputs = model(**encoding)
    return {"method": "layoutlmv3", "logits": outputs.logits.tolist()}
Layout is a first-class input feature
: LayoutLMv3 encodes each OCR token's bounding box as a 2D positional embedding (x0, y0, x1, y1 × 1000). Without this, a model cannot distinguish a table header from a body cell with identical text.

: in OCR-dependent pipelines, a misread character in a key field ("$1.42" instead of "$142") propagates uncorrected to the final extraction. Use high-quality OCR engines and consider post-correction steps.
Donut eliminates OCR but requires more fine-tuning data
: the model must learn to read text and understand layout simultaneously. Typically 500–2000 labelled documents are needed for good performance on a new domain.
GPT-4o is the fastest path for diverse document types
: zero-shot, handles merged cells, rotated text, and mixed layouts. Cost and privacy are the trade-offs.
: if the PDF was digitally created (not scanned), pdfplumber extracts text and tables with exact coordinates — faster and more accurate than any ML model.
ANLS is the standard DocVQA metric
: it normalises for minor OCR differences (spacing, punctuation) by treating predictions with Levenshtein similarity < 0.5 as completely wrong.
